justification: Ch3 argues Jesus's titles derive from Hellenistic frameworks and ch4 discusses the Dionysian Gospel (ch4 inv[35]). However, the specific Revelation wine-press/Dionysus parallel (treading grapes, purple imagery, intoxication) is not in ch3's inventory. The Greco-Christian titulature argument exists (ch3 inv[2]), but this specific Dionysian-Revelation imagery is new evidence.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos as Greek philosophical concept (Heraclitus, Stoics, Philo) and ch3 inv[9] covers John's Logos. However, the specific argument about translation choices masking Greek theological distinctions (Logos, Agape, Zoe as systematically flattened in English) is not presented in ch3. The argument that Greek concepts are obscured by translation is new evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Messiah as authentic Judean royal title and inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms. The specific point about 'anointed' carrying variable political meanings (royal, priestly, prophetic) depending on context is a nuance not explicitly in the inventory, though the general argument about Christos as political title is present.

The Parable of the Pounds (Luke 19) as embedded in the Archelaus succession crisis is not in ch3's inventory. Ch3 covers collective sin and kingdom restoration (inv[45-46]) and political language, but the specific argument that Jesus directly engaged Herodian political-dynastic theology through the Parable of the Pounds is neither the argument nor the evidence in ch3.

Ch3 inv[2] argues titles were pre-existing political terms and inv[32] covers Kyrios. The book does not specifically discuss Melchizedek as a royal title meaning 'righteous king' in the context of Hellenistic titulature. Psalm 110 is referenced (inv[19]) but as part of the Father/Kyrios distinction, not as evidence for Hellenistic royal titulature via Melchizedek. New evidence for the existing titulature argument.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Messiah/Christos as Greek royal title and inv[2] covers pre-existing titles. Eshmon as an anointing deity syncretized with Adonis and Asclepius, suggesting Christ's 'Anointed One' title draws from Hellenistic anointing protocols, is new specific evidence not in the inventory. The argument that 'Christ' derives from broader Mediterranean anointing tradition is already present.

Ch3 does not have a dedicated dying-and-rising gods section, but the chapter text discusses resurrection as political-theological legitimacy. The specific catalog of multiple dying-and-rising gods (Tamuz, Osiris, Attis, Dionysus, Adonis, Orpheus) with 3-day motifs at spring equinox is not in ch3 inventory. The broader Greco-Christian framework argument exists. Ch4 inv[35] discusses the Dionysian Gospel. This is new evidence for the existing framework.

Ch3 inv[5] covers Justin Martyr's argument about sons of Zeus including Heracles and Dionysus. Simon Magus described as 'son of Jupiter' and compared to Heracles is a new specific example not in the inventory, bolstering the existing comparative titulature argument.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos theology (Heraclitus, Stoics, Philo) and inv[9] covers Logos in John. Hebrews' high christology describing Jesus as 'the one through whom god has created the universe' and its logos/wisdom theology connection is new specific textual evidence not in the ch3 inventory, supporting the existing Greco-philosophical framework argument.

Ch3 inv[40] covers Jesus's birth as a new star with Augustus's star/comet and Alexander's star trope. Adair's specific examples of Mithridates VI with comets at birth/coronation and Augustus's explicit propaganda about Caesar's comet on coinage add new comparative detail to the existing celestial-legitimacy argument already in the inventory.

Ch3 inv[50] covers Mary carrying Greek royal imagery and titles. The specific theotokos debate at Council of Ephesus (431 CE) between Cyril and Nestorius, showing Greek divine categories required elevating Mary to goddess status, is new evidence not in the inventory. The argument about Mary in Greek royal framework already exists.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology and inv[9] covers the Father described in Greek categories. Philo's specific Eucharistic mysticism and 'cup bearer of the Eucharist' language is not in ch3 inventory. The broader argument about Greco-Jewish theological frameworks predating Christianity is present.

Ch3 does not discuss the Attis festival's death-and-resurrection motif (fastened to tree, buried three days, celebrated) or John the Lydian's dating evidence for the 40-140 AD period. Neither this specific evidence nor the argument about Greek mystery religion precedent for resurrection theology appears in ch3 inventory. Ch4 inv[35] mentions the Dionysian Gospel but not Attis specifically.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and inv[25] covers 'Son of God' as standard Hellenistic title. The chapter text states: 'Augustus presented himself as divi filius, son of the divine Julius.' Augustus as a divinely-chosen messianic figure establishing 'Son of God' as Hellenistic royal title is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[6] covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture at annunciation, baptism, and resurrection. The specific evidence that Epiphany (January 6) originally celebrated adoptionist belief that Jesus was 'adopted as God's son at the Jordan baptism' is not in ch3 inventory. The argument about baptism as investiture/acquired status exists; the Epiphany liturgical evidence is new.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology and inv[9] covers the Father described in Greek philosophical categories. The specific parallel between Paul's theology of Jesus and Philo's theology of the archangel as 'firstborn son of god' with identical terms (image of god, paraclete, logos) is new evidence not explicitly in the inventory, bolstering the existing Logos/Philo argument.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and inv[5] covers Justin Martyr on sons of Zeus. The specific tracing of how Matthew increasingly adopted Hellenistic pagan birth narratives while Mark retained Jewish adoption model (showing progressive Greco-Christian synthesis across gospels) is new evidence for the existing Hellenistic titulature argument.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek word meaning 'anointed' and ch2 inv[29] covers Christos as Greek royal title. The specific claim that 'christos' derives from Greek mystery religion meaning 'the one stung by the gadfly' connected to ecstatic mania is a new etymological/cultic evidence not in either inventory. The argument about Christos as Greek term exists.

Ch3 inv[2] argues titles were pre-existing political terms of Greek kingship. The specific claim that 'Son of God' was essential for religious credibility and competitive viability in the Hellenistic marketplace ('if you weren't called the son of god your game in town was useless') is a new framing/evidence point not in inventory, supporting the existing argument.

Ch3 inv[35] covers Alexander claiming divine sonship through Zeus-Ammon at Siwa. The chapter text explicitly states: 'Alexander the Great claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon, and his image appeared with the horns of Ammon on coins.' This finding repeats that Alexander was 'Son of God' as a Hellenistic royal title, which is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology (Philo as Jewish aristocrat, Logos as rational cosmic principle). The specific detail of Philo's emanationist stages of the logos (mind of God within God, blueprint of creation, heavenly Adam as living blueprint, 'almost gnostic emanationism') adds new specific detail not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument.

Ch4 inv[35] discusses John as a 'Dionysian Gospel' built on imitation of Euripides' Bacchae. Ch3 does not specifically discuss Jesus appropriating Dionysian imagery ('I am the True Vine') or the water-to-wine miracle as Dionysian. Ch3 inv[2] lists titles as pre-existing terms. The specific Dionysian vine/wine parallels are new evidence for the existing Greco-Christian framework argument in ch3.

Ch3 does not argue that the Trinity derives from Platonic traditions connected to Dionysian/Zoroastrian theology. Ch3 inv[9] covers God the Father described in Greek categories and inv[10] covers Gnostics/Marcionites, but no inventory item makes the specific argument about Trinitarian doctrine's Platonic-Dionysian origin. Both the argument and the Saturn-Zeus-Dionysus triad evidence are new to ch3.

Ch3 inv[5] covers Justin Martyr's argument equating Jesus's divine sonship with sons of Zeus. The specific evidence of a second-century Christian (Naassene preacher) singing a hymn identifying Jesus with Kronos, Zeus, Dionysus, Rhea, and Adonis is new evidence not in ch3 inventory. The argument about early Christians using Greek theological categories exists.

The Zalmoxis narrative (disappeared underground for three years, reappeared in the fourth, validated his teaching about immortality, 500 years before Christianity) is not in ch3 inventory. Ch3 does not make an argument about resurrection as political-theological legitimacy claim using Zalmoxis as comparative evidence. Both the specific Herodotean Zalmoxis evidence and the resurrection-as-validation argument are new to ch3.

Ch3 inv[3] covers Hasmonean dynasty adopting Hellenistic royal theology and mentions John Hyrcanus. The specific point that John Hyrcanus was the first to combine Prophet, Priest, and King (from Tabor via Josephus) is not in the inventory. The Hasmonean Hellenistic royal theology argument exists; this specific triple-title precedent is new evidence.

Ch3 inv[35] explicitly covers 'Alexander the Great claimed divine sonship through Zeus-Ammon at Siwa.' The chapter text states: 'Alexander the Great claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon, and his image appeared with the horns of Ammon on coins.' The Alexander Romance presenting Alexander as Son of God born of Amon through divine impregnation is the same evidence.

Ch3 inv[35] covers Alexander's divine sonship claim. The specific detail of kingship legitimated through oracles and divine approval from multiple god-names (Amon, Serapis, Zeus simultaneously) and Alexander dedicating a sanctuary to 'his father the god Amon' adds new specific textual evidence to the existing Alexander argument.

Ch3 inv[40] covers Jesus's birth as new star and lists 'Augustus: birth represented as new star; death/deification of Julius Caesar.' The detailed Augustus birth narrative parallels (divine kingship through miraculous birth, threatened infanticide, Son of God status as standard literary trope) add new specific narrative detail to the existing argument about celestial/birth legitimacy claims.

Ch3 inv[31] covers Soter as the most common title for Hellenistic rulers. Evidence of 'savior god' concepts 700 years before Jesus (Apollo as salvific God, Ashmanaser = 'Ashman saves') provides new specific pre-Christian examples not in the inventory, supporting the existing Soter/savior argument.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly covers 'Son of God' as pre-existing political term and lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and 'Cities hailed emperors as soter.' The chapter text discusses the Caesar cult context. This finding's argument that 'Son of God' carried specific political meaning tied to Caesar worship is directly covered in the opening section.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and the chapter text discusses how Greeks heard 'vocabulary of sovereignty reassigned.' The specific observation that NT Greek uses 'kingdom of arenos' for heaven, references Poseidon and Hades, and sounds like Homer/Virgil to Greek pagans is new evidence not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument about Greek political-religious idiom.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and inv[35] covers Alexander's divine sonship. The chapter text describes Julius Caesar's deification and Augustus as 'son of the divine Julius.' This finding repeats the same examples (Roman emperors' deification, heavenly ascension, Son of God title) already covered.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek anointing term. The specific Odyssey parallel (Jesus's anointing in Gospels mirrors Odysseus's anointing, Eurocleia = 'fame far and wide' as pun in Mark) is not in ch3 inventory. This is new literary evidence for the existing Greek anointing argument. Note: appears repeatedly in multiple findings in this batch (FEU04YG1aGQ_1, V6shchstY9k_1, lA-70CJlmkA_0).

Ch3 inv[5] covers Justin Martyr naming Dionysus among sons of Zeus. The specific evidence that Dionysus was called 'the son of god' in the Bacchae (c. 400 BCE) with the opening line 'the son of god has entered the world' is a new specific textual reference not in the inventory, supporting the existing comparative titulature argument.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and the chapter text discusses Augustus as divi filius. The specific discussion of Christ's resurrection as apotheosis/exaltation/divinization paralleled to Romulus myths and Romans 1:4 ('became Son of God in power at resurrection') is new evidence connecting resurrection to political apotheosis, supporting the existing Hellenistic framework argument.

Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek divine categories (Justin Martyr, Theophilus). Clement of Alexandria portraying Jesus as 'the true Orpheus' in the Protrepticus is a new specific patristic source not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument about early Christians using Greek theological categories.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos theology (Heraclitus, Stoics, Philo). Hermes Trismegistus as a Logos figure with creator attributes paralleling Johannine Logos theology is a new specific Hellenistic Logos parallel not in the inventory. The argument about Greek Logos frameworks predating Christianity exists.

Ch3 inv[11] covers Jesus teaching the Greek/Platonic model of the immortal soul. The Acts of Andrew presenting an apostle as 'christianized Socrates' using Plato's Phaedo language about the soul's release from the body is new evidence of early Christian Platonic theology not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument about Greek philosophical frameworks.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos/anointing as a political title. The specific Isis-Osiris anointing parallel (Mary Magdalene as priestess enabling sacred transformation through anointing, like Isis anointing Osiris) is new evidence not in ch3 inventory. The argument about anointing in Greek/Mediterranean royal tradition exists.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as anointing title. The specific Homer's Odyssey / Gospel anointing parallel (Eurocleia = 'fame far and wide', Jesus tells woman 'her fame will be remembered far and wide' as deliberate pun) is new literary evidence not in the inventory. Same evidence as C-qXmlzm28Y_1.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology and its connection to Jesus as Son of God. The specific mechanism described (Philo mapping Logos onto 'Son of God' through hypothesizing distinct divine moments) adds new detail about how the one-to-one mapping worked, supporting the existing Philo/Logos argument.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Stoics (Zeno, Chrysippus) developing Logos as rational cosmic principle. The specific tracing of Stoic logos through Hermeticists to Christianity by the third century ('stoicizing version of the logos had taken root... is what the Christians inherit') adds historical transmission detail not in the inventory.

Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek divine sonship categories. The specific second-century Naassene hymn to Attis applied to Jesus (identifying Jesus with Osiris, Adonis, son of Zeus) is new evidence of early Christians explicitly equating Jesus with Greco-Roman dying-rising gods. The argument about comparative titulature exists.

Ch3 does not discuss Matthew 19:12 (castration saying) as reflecting Attis cult castration symbolism. Neither the specific evidence (Matthew 19:12 interpreted through Attis cult) nor the argument (Jesus's words understood through mystery religion frameworks) appears in ch3 inventory. This is a new and potentially controversial argument with new evidence.

Ch3 inv[31] explicitly covers 'Soter (Savior) was the most common title for Hellenistic rulers, used across Ptolemaic, Seleucid, and other dynasties' with evidence including Ptolemy I Soter, Antiochus I Soter. The chapter text states: 'Cities hailed emperors as soter (savior) for providing grain, protection, and peace.' This finding about prevalence of Soter epithet is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[5] explicitly covers Justin Martyr naming Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, and Perseus as sons of Zeus alongside Jesus. Ch3 inv[2] lists Alexander's Zeus-Ammon claim. Zeus fathering Heracles and Dionysus as divine birth patterns paralleling Jesus is directly covered by the existing Justin Martyr and title evidence.

Ch3 does not discuss Dionysus's specific death-and-resurrection narrative in Orphic mysteries. While ch3 argues about Hellenistic titles and Greek categories, it does not make the specific argument about dying-and-rising as central to mystery religion theology as a path to divine status. The Orphic Dionysus death-rebirth narrative evidence is new to ch3.

Ch3 does not argue that the Trinity derives from Egyptian/Greek Father-Mother-Son family models rather than Jewish theology. Ch3 inv[9] covers Father described in Greek categories but not Trinitarian origin. The Sethian Father-Mother-Son pattern as Trinity origin is both a new argument and new evidence for ch3.

Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek divine categories. The Simonians (worshipping Simon as Zeus, Helena as Aphrodite/Athena) and Carpocratians (comparing Jesus to Pythagoras and Plato with statues of all three) are new specific examples of early Christian sects using Greek theological categories, supporting the existing argument.

Ch3 inv[32] covers Kyrios as standard imperial honorific and inv[19] covers the Father/Kyrios distinction. The specific Alexandrian Jewish interpretation where kurios functions as both divine title (for YHWH in LXX) and imperial title (for Roman emperors) is a new nuance from Dr. Lillie not explicitly in the inventory, supporting the existing Kyrios argument.

Ch3 inv[10] covers Gnostics viewing God of Moses as lower/ignorant power. The specific Yaldabaoth example (claiming 'I'm god and no one else exists beside me' as parallel to Roman emperors' self-divinization) is a new framing from Dr. Lillie connecting Gnostic theology to imperial critique. The argument exists; the specific imperial-critique framing is new evidence.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly covers 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and the chapter text states: 'Augustus presented himself as divi filius.' The finding about Augustus's deification as Son of God and the Roman imperial cult is directly covered in ch3's opening section.

Ch3 inv[6] covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture at baptism and resurrection. Romans 1:4 (Jesus 'declared to be the son of god in power by his resurrection') as adoptionist Christology is not explicitly cited in ch3 inventory, though Acts 13:33 is. This specific Pauline text is new evidence for the existing investiture/acquired-status argument.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists Alexander's Zeus-Ammon claim with coins and inv[35] covers Alexander at Siwa. The chapter text names 'Alexander the Great claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon, and his image appeared with the horns of Ammon on coins.' Augustus as divi filius on coins is also explicitly listed. The 'ubiquitous Hellenistic royal title' argument with these exact examples is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology. The specific framing of Philo's project as 'syncretizing Judaism with pagan concepts' including Pythagoreanism and Platonism, where 'the logos is going to inherit the kingdom of heaven,' adds new detail about Philo's syncretic method not explicitly in the inventory.

Ch3 does not discuss Egyptian bodily resurrection as proof of divine legitimacy. The specific argument about Osiris's physical resurrection functioning as 'miraculous proof of divinity' paralleling Jesus's resurrection (including Thomas's doubt) is not in ch3 inventory. While ch3 discusses titles and political theology, it does not make this specific resurrection-as-divine-proof argument using Egyptian evidence.

Ch3 inv[25] covers 'Son of God' as standard Hellenistic title derived from Egyptian 'Son of Ra.' Cooney's tracing of the title across Osiris, Horus, and Jesus as cross-cultural divine/royal designation involving virgin birth and divine conception adds Egyptian religious tradition detail not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek anointing term. The specific Eshmun material connecting Mediterranean anointing ceremonies to kingship ritual (Phoenician kings anointed with oil) is new evidence from a specific Mediterranean tradition not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument about Christ/anointing in Hellenistic royal tradition.

Ch3 inv[25] covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title and inv[2] lists various pre-existing titles. Eshmun as a pre-Christian 'Son of God' who is a dying-rising deity, healer, and anointed figure ('offspring of the mother queen of heaven and the lord father baal') is new specific Mediterranean evidence not in the inventory.

Ch3 does not discuss pre-Christian baptismal parallels in Mediterranean temple contexts. Eshmun temples featuring ritual ablutions in sacred basins fed by running water as parallel to Christian baptismal practice is both a new argument (pre-Christian Mediterranean baptismal parallels) and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and the chapter text discusses Augustus as 'son of the divine Julius.' The chapter also mentions 'Cities hailed emperors as soter.' Berman's examples (Augustus designated Son of God, deified, ascending, spitting to heal blind like Vespasian) repeat evidence already in the chapter. The Vespasian miracle parallel is new detail but the core Son of God/Augustus argument is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek categories (Justin Martyr). The Naassene hymn repurposing Attis hymn for Jesus (identifying him as Kronos, Zeus, Rhea, Adonis, Osiris) is new specific patristic evidence not in the inventory. The argument about Christians using Hellenistic divine categories exists; this is another instance.

Ch3 inv[35] covers Alexander claiming divine sonship through Zeus-Ammon and the chapter text explicitly parallels Alexander's claim to Jesus's. The specific parallel of 'my Father' language (Alexander to Zeus, Jesus in Gospels) is implicit in the existing coverage of Alexander's divine sonship. The core evidence (Alexander as Son of God, same as Jesus) is covered.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists Augustus as divi filius on coins and ch3 inv[35] covers Alexander at Siwa. The chapter text discusses both. Augustus styling himself as 'son of god' after Caesar's deification with numismatic evidence is directly covered in the opening section.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and inv[33] covers Prince of Peace. The specific framing of Augustus implementing pax romana as political-theological salvation doctrine decades before Jesus, establishing Greek framework of 'political messianism,' adds the comparative salvific-political framework detail. The Philo writing about Augustus 'calming storms' is new evidence not in inventory.

Ch3 inv[2] covers Son of God as pre-existing political title with Augustus as divi filius. Titus as son of the deified Vespasian being recognized as 'son of god' in the same region and era as Christianity, plus the Josephus 'the son comes' passage, is new specific evidence not in the inventory, supporting the existing comparative titulature argument.

Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek divine categories. The Naassene preacher substituting Jesus's name for Attis/Osiris/Dionysus/Adonis in hymns is repeated across multiple findings in this batch. While the Naassene evidence appears in multiple findings, it is not in ch3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument about early Christians using Greek categories.

Ch3 inv[14] covers Egyptian solar-royal culture alive in the 1st century and discusses solar imagery. The specific connection between Hellenistic solar monotheism (Helios/Elagabalus) and early Christianity's institutional formation is new evidence. The argument about solar-royal continuity exists; the specific Helios/Elagabalus-to-Christianity institutional link is new.

Ch3 inv[28] covers Jesus as 'God manifested in the flesh' (Epiphanes). Glover's analysis of epiphany scenes (transfiguration, empty tomb) as Greco-Roman literary-theological motifs for justifying cultic worship and new priesthood is new evidence framing these Gospel episodes through Greek epiphany conventions not in the inventory.

Ch3 inv[5] explicitly covers Justin Martyr, First Apology 21-22, stating Christians 'propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus.' The finding quotes virtually the same passage from Justin Martyr about the logos, firstborn of God, crucifixion, death, resurrection, and 'sons of Zeus.' This is the same evidence already in the inventory.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos theology from Heraclitus, Stoics, and Philo. The specific evidence that Greek philosophers identified various divine figures (Heracles, Dionysus, Zeus) as the Logos/'firstborn of God'/'second god' is new specific textual evidence showing Logos was applied to multiple deities, supporting the existing Logos argument.

Ch3 inv[27] explicitly covers Philo's Logos theology including 'Philo of Alexandria: Jewish aristocrat' developing Logos. The chapter text discusses Philo's doctrine of the Logos. Philo identifying the logos as 'firstborn of God' and 'son of God' is directly covered by the existing Philo material in the inventory.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and mentions Augustus on coins. Ch2 inv[30] covers euangelion as political term. The specific Priene Calendar inscription (9 BC) using the Greek term evangelion for Augustus as 'savior' is not in ch3 inventory. The argument about Greek political-theological vocabulary exists; this specific inscription is new evidence.

Ch3 inv[41] covers Basileia tou Theou as technical term for Hellenistic divine monarchies. The observation about competing messianic movements in both Judaism and Roman imperialism indicating 'kingdom' and 'salvation' language was fundamentally political-theological supports the existing argument but without new specific evidence beyond what is already covered.

Ch3 inv[9] covers John's Gospel presenting Jesus as Logos from Greek philosophy. The specific Platonic triadic structure (monad, diad, triad) mapped onto John 1:1-3, with Zoe connected to Eve/creation as a third figure forming a Trinity, is new philosophical-structural evidence not in the inventory, supporting the existing Logos/Greek framework argument.

Ch3 inv[31] covers Soter as Hellenistic title and inv[5] mentions Dionysus among sons of Zeus. The specific parallel of Dionysus and Jesus both uniquely demanding 'recognition' (belief and worship) as a Thracian concept is new evidence about a specific theological parallel not in the inventory.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms. The specific parallel between Helios/Apollo/Mithras as mediating figures and Christian Logos theology, with Julian's depiction of the sun as mediator competing with the Jesus-Yahweh dyad, is new evidence not in the inventory, supporting the existing Hellenistic framework argument.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms. Plutarch's comparison of Mithra as mediator in Zoroastrianism paralleling Christ as mediator in the NT is a new specific cross-tradition comparison not in the inventory. The argument about pre-existing titles exists; the Mithra/mediator parallel is new evidence.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos theology from Heraclitus, Stoics, and Philo. Plutarch describing Osiris as the 'logos' of heaven and the lower world is a new specific source showing Logos applied to non-Christian deities, supporting the existing argument that Logos was established Hellenistic vocabulary.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos theology and inv[9] covers John's Logos. MacDonald's specific parallel between John's Logos prologue and the opening monologue of Euripides' Bacchae (Dionysus claiming divine sonship through his mother) is new literary evidence. Ch4 inv[35] mentions John as 'Dionysian Gospel' built on Bacchae, but in ch3 this specific Bacchae-John parallel is new evidence.

Ch3 does not discuss Q source or Homeric mimesis in parables. The specific example of Q imitating Homer's Odyssey (parable of servants paralleling Odysseus's servants, Melanthius eating with suitors, Odysseus chopping them up) is both a new argument (Q source literary dependence on Homer) and new evidence not in ch3 inventory. More relevant to ch4 (gospel composition).

MacDonald's 'social identity criticism' methodology distinguishing Greek mythologizing layers from Jewish institutional material in the gospels is primarily about gospel composition methodology, which belongs in ch4 (Gospels as Historically Reliable Sources). Ch4 already discusses gospel composition and literary techniques.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek anointing title. David's anointing described as 'cup-bearer' (kalim) to Saul paralleling Ganymede in Greek tradition is a new specific cross-cultural anointing parallel not in the inventory, supporting the existing Greek anointing argument.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and inv[28] covers 'God manifested in the flesh.' The precise Greco-Roman definition of deification (immortal life, answering prayer, power on earth, superhuman body) as matching exactly the attributes ascribed to Jesus is new specific definitional evidence not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument.

Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek categories. Clement of Alexandria conforming Christ to Orpheus ('new Orpheus singing the new song of salvation') in the Protrepticus is new specific patristic evidence not in the inventory. Note: The chapter text mentions Clement of Alexandria in a different context (Stromata 5.12 about universal Father).

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles framed in Greek political-religious idiom. Walsh's observation that Matthew portrays Jesus as a Stoic philosopher while simultaneously drawing on Jewish scripture (dual framing for different audiences) is new evidence about compositional strategy not in the inventory, supporting the existing Greco-Christian framework argument.

Ch3 inv[27] explicitly covers 'Philo of Alexandria: Jewish aristocrat; brother Alexander the Alabarch' and describes Philo as a Middle Platonist who synthesized Jewish scripture with Greek philosophy. The chapter text describes 'Alexandrian Judaism, represented above all by Philo.' Walsh's characterization of Philo as Middle Platonist integrating Greek philosophy while maintaining Jewish identity is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology. The specific trinity of God, Sophia, and the Lord within a Platonic framework, with logos described as the sun, is new specific detail about Philo's trinitarian structure not in the inventory, supporting the existing Philo/Logos argument.

Ch3 does not discuss the intelligible triad (being, life, intellect) from Porphyry into Church Father theology or the feminine nature of the Holy Spirit's middle position. This Neoplatonic-to-Trinitarian transmission with the feminine principle is both a new argument and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.

Ch3 does not discuss Eudorus of Alexandria or his Platonic monad/diad proto-Trinitarian structure. This pre-Christian Alexandrian philosophical theology directly prefiguring Trinity doctrine is both a new argument and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.

Ch3 inv[9] covers Gospel of John presenting Jesus as Logos from Greek philosophy. The specific Platonic triadic structure in John 1 (logos, Zoe/life as female principle connected to Eve via LXX) adds new philosophical-structural evidence not in the inventory, supporting the existing argument about John's Greek framework.

Ch3 inv[25] covers 'Son of God' as standard Hellenistic title derived from Egyptian pharaonic tradition. The specific point that OT kings of Israel were called 'Son of God' (making it a royal designation, not a claim of divine equality) is a new specific piece of evidence supporting the existing argument about Son of God as political-royal category.

Ch3 inv[41] covers Basileia tou Theou as Hellenistic divine monarchy term. The parallel between Christian sacramental initiation (baptism, Eucharist, faith-based salvation) and Greek mystery initiation is new evidence. The Q&A global decision rejects 'mystery cult' framing but says to extract institutional data and reframe through imperial cult. This evidence should be reframed accordingly.

Ch3 inv[9] covers John's Logos from Greek philosophy. Ch4 inv[35] discusses John as 'Dionysian Gospel.' The specific portrayal of Jesus in John as a 'philosophical cynic stoic type of thinker' resembling the Platonic philosopher-Bacchus (not the ecstatic Dionysus) is new evidence about John's specific philosophical characterization not in ch3 inventory.

Ch3 does not discuss the Eleusinian Mysteries' ritual consumption of 'body and blood' paralleling the Eucharist, or the Dionysian dismemberment-and-reconstitution mirroring resurrection theology. The Q&A global decision rejects 'mystery cult' framing but says to extract data. The specific Eleusinian body-and-blood evidence is new. However, this may be better placed in ch4 which discusses John omitting the Eucharist because it was already an established Dionysian rite (ch4 inv[15]).

Ch3 inv[27] explicitly covers 'Philo of Alexandria: Jewish aristocrat' who 'synthesized Jewish scripture with Greek philosophy and developed the concept of the Logos.' The chapter text states the same. Philo as the crucial figure synthesizing Greek philosophy with Jewish thought to create the Logos concept that Christians inherited is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Philo's Logos theology with Heraclitus and Stoics. Aristobulus of Alexandria as a pre-Philo Jewish philosopher from the Aristotelian school who similarly synthesized Greek Logos concepts with Judaism is new historical evidence of an earlier figure in the same tradition, supporting the existing argument about Hellenistic philosophical inheritance.

Ch3 inv[27] covers Logos theology from various Greek traditions. Hermes holding identical titles to Christ (logos, word of god, messenger, mediator) is new specific evidence of a Greek deity bearing the same theological titles, supporting the existing Logos argument.

Ch3 inv[2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms and the chapter discusses how Greek rule was 'by nature divine.' Plato's specific prescription that rulers must convince citizens 'their laws were given by a god' as the operative framework for Hellenistic kingdoms is a new specific philosophical source not in the inventory, supporting the existing political theology argument.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek anointing term. The specific connection of Christos to Greek mystery religion practice (healing cults, oil for immortality, 'oystermania' initiatory state) and to Greco-religious healer deities (Iasos, Addis, Ashman) is new evidence about the cultic dimension of the anointing concept not in the inventory.

Ch3 does not discuss gematria or Pythagorean-Orphic numerical theology applied to Jesus. The specific argument about 888 as gematria of Jesus derived from Greek mathematical mysticism (ogdoad generating numeric sequences) is both a new argument and new evidence not in any chapter inventory.

Iasos/Yasios from Samothracian mysteries as a dying-and-rising healer deity with linguistic roots shared with Jesus is not in ch3 inventory. Neither the specific evidence (Iasos struck down by Zeus, brought to Hades, resurrected) nor the argument (direct mythological precedent for Jesus narrative in Greek tradition) appears in ch3.

Ch3 inv[10] covers Gnostics viewing God of Moses as lower power. The specific claim that early Gnostic Christians understood Jesus as son of Sophia rather than Yahweh, documented by Hippolytus, adds new specific evidence about early Gnostic Christology. The argument that earliest proclamation was received in Greek categories (not tied to YHWH) exists in ch3.

Ch3 does not discuss the transformation of dying-and-rising god myths from agricultural symbolism to soteriological theology in urban Hellenistic contexts. The specific argument about urbanization changing mystery religion meaning from agricultural cycle to 'liberation or deification guaranteeing eternal life' is both a new argument and new evidence for ch3.

Ch3 inv[5] covers sons of Zeus including Dionysus. Ch4 inv[35] discusses Dionysian Gospel. The specific parallel between Dionysus and Jesus both demanding 'recognition' (belief and worship) as their sole concern is new evidence about a specific theological parallel. The argument about Jesus framed in Greek divine categories exists.

Ch3 does not discuss the 12 disciples as parallel to 12 zodiac signs or as 'new tribal patriarchs.' This Greco-astrological interpretation of the 12 apostles is both a new argument and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.

The Infancy Gospel of Thomas portraying Jesus with divine creative powers is about apocryphal gospel traditions and Jesus's childhood miracles. This does not bear on any argument in ch3 (which covers titles, political theology, kingdom language). The apocryphal traditions are more relevant to ch4 (gospel reliability) if at all.

Ch3 inv[50] covers Mary carrying Greek royal imagery. Ch3 inv[10] covers Gnostics and their theological positions. Litwa's identification of Helen as Simon's 'Divine thought' compared to Athena from Zeus's head, showing Simonian theology used Greco-Roman divine frameworks, is new specific evidence supporting the existing argument about Greek categories in early Christian theology.

Simon designated with 'God above all rule Authority and power' (paralleling Ephesians) and the statue base connecting Simon to Simo Sancus (Greco-Italic deity), with some claiming he was 'son of Jupiter,' is new evidence. The specific argument about early Christian figures understood through Greco-Roman divine kingship rather than Jewish prophetic models at this level of detail is new to ch3.

Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek word meaning 'anointed.' Demeter calling Demiphon 'christos' in the Homeric Hymn to Demeter is a new specific pre-Christian Greek source using the exact term 'christos' for anointing, not in the inventory. This is strong new evidence for the existing argument.

Ch3 does not discuss Eleusinian mystery theology as precedent for Christian resurrection soteriology. Ch3 inv[32] covers Kyrios as standard imperial honorific and inv[31] covers Soter. Ch3 inv[5] covers early Christian apologists using Greek categories. Ch3 inv[35] explicitly covers Alexander declared son of God at the oracle of Ammon, and the chapter text states: 'Alexander the Great claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon.' Ch3 inv[5] explicitly covers Justin Martyr's First Apology 21-22 acknowledging Jesus must compete with sons of Zeus and addressing the charge. Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals.' Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek anointing term. Ch3 inv[24] covers Christos as Greek anointing term. Ch3 inv[5] covers sons of Zeus including Heracles and Dionysus. Ch3 inv[5] explicitly covers Justin Martyr's First Apology 21-22 stating Christians 'propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus' naming Mercury/Hermes and Asclepius. Ch3 inv[5] covers sons of Zeus including Dionysus. Ch3 inv[28] covers Jesus as Epiphanes/Emmanuel. Ch3 inv[2] explicitly lists 'Alexander claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon' and 'Augustus as divi filius on coins.' The chapter text names Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, and Perseus. Ch3 does not discuss Asclepius's healing miracles as comparative evidence to Jesus. Ch3 inv[40] covers Jesus's birth as new star and mentions Augustus's star/deification. Ch3 does not discuss 'road encounters' as a standard Greco-Roman religious trope. The argument that the Road to Emmaus narrative follows established Roman road-encounter literary patterns (people seeing Caesars on roads) is both a new argument and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.

Ch3 inv[35] explicitly covers 'Alexander the Great claimed divine sonship through Zeus-Ammon at Siwa' with 'Oracle at Siwa in Egypt (Plutarch, Lives 27).' This finding about Alexander proclaimed 'son of Ammon' is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[2] covers Augustus as divi filius and the chapter text discusses how 'Augustus presented himself as divi filius, son of the divine Julius.' Ch3 inv[5] covers sons of Zeus including Dionysus. Ch3 inv[27] explicitly covers 'Philo of Alexandria: Jewish aristocrat' who developed Logos theology before NT. The chapter text describes Philo's Logos doctrine. Philo believing in the Logos as divine 'Son of God' prior to Christianity is directly covered.

Ch3 inv[2] explicitly covers 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals.' The chapter text says the same. Rabbi Singer's discussion of Julius Caesar as son of Venus and Augustus styling himself as son of god on coins is the same evidence already covered.

Ch3 inv[34] covers 'Light of the World' relating to solar metaphors. 'Sol Invictus' (unconquerable sun) as a title of Mithra predating Christian adaptation, used in Plato, is new evidence about a specific solar title's transmission from pagan to Christian usage, supporting the existing solar metaphor argument.

Ch3 does not discuss Zeus-Jehovah connections or Sabazios/Sabaoth etymological links (Macrobius thinking Sabazeus is a cognate with Jehovah Sabaoth). This is a new specific argument about deity identification/syncretism and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.

Ch3 does not discuss Adam as a four-lettered Greek cosmological concept connected to Samothracian mysteries. The Sibyl saying Adam is a 'tetragrammaton' meaning north/south/east/west as a Greek magical practice with Samothracian origins is both a new argument and new evidence not in ch3 inventory.
